nativeRange.habitat: Forest streams, slow-moving minor tributaries, igapó and igarapé flooded forest ecosystems; blackwater with tannin-stained, highly acidic, very soft water

diet.notes: Micropredator omnivore that tends to accept most small prepared foods readily, including flakes, nano/micropellets, freeze-dried tubifex, and frozen foods such as baby brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, and bloodworm. Live foods are generally relished.

summary: An iconic Amazonian schooling fish prized for its vivid full-length red belly and brilliant iridescent blue stripe. Generally considered intermediate in difficulty due to sensitivity to poor water quality and a preference for soft, acidic conditions.
careNotes: Cardinal tetras tend to do best in mature, well-filtered aquariums with stable water chemistry — sudden parameter swings are generally more harmful than absolute values. Wild-caught specimens are significantly more demanding than captive-bred individuals and often require pH well below 6.0 with very soft water to acclimate properly. Captive-bred stock is considerably hardier and more tolerant of slightly harder, more neutral water (up to ~pH 7.0 and ~10 dGH). A school of at least 8 is advisable for natural schooling behavior and reduced stress; 10–15 is preferable in larger setups. The species is commonly confused with the Neon Tetra (Paracheirodon innesi), which is smaller and has red colouration only on the rear half of the body — the Cardinal's red extends the full body length. Note on taxonomy: FishBase (2024 onward) places this species in family Acestrorhamphidae rather than the traditional Characidae; most aquarium hobby sources and SeriouslyFish still list Characidae.
breedingNotes: Reported to be advanced in captivity. Breeding has been documented but is considered challenging: a dedicated species tank is generally needed with very soft (near-zero hardness), acidic water (pH 5.0–6.0), dim or blackout lighting, and a temperature drop of a few degrees to trigger spawning. Spawning tends to occur nocturnally (often around midnight). Eggs are light-sensitive and hatch in roughly 24–36 hours; fry become free-swimming within 3–4 days. Raising fry is considered the hardest part as they require infusoria and very fine foods. Most aquarium Cardinal Tetras are wild-caught from the Rio Negro.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, water parameters, native range, tank dimensions, and breeding notes. Hardness given as 18–215 ppm (≈1–12 dGH); lower end recommended for wild/breeding stock. pH 3.5–7.5 with lower end preferred.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 3.0 cm SL, temperature 23–27°C, pH 4.0–6.0, native range Upper Orinoco and Negro River basins. FishBase classifies family as Acestrorhamphidae (post-reclassification); hobby sources retain Characidae.

fish.breedingNotes: Requires dedicated breeding tank with very soft, acidic blackwater conditions, dim/blackout lighting, and slight temperature reduction. Nocturnal spawner; eggs light-sensitive.
